Hi, I'm Emily, thanks so much for reaching out, and I will go ahead and answer the questions that you provided me. The first one is asking this role reviews every activity in a lesson for errors before it reaches a classroom. Walk us through how you would systematically review a single K to 12 lesson. What kind of errors would you look for and how would you keep your process consistent across many lessons? I would definitely take my time, I would not rush through it, I would go through it line by line to make sure that all of the formatting was correct, as well as the spelling and the grammar. I would keep my process consistent by taking my time and working off a checklist to make sure that I wasn't missing anything that needed to be checked. Tell us your experience about catching content or quality errors in educational or digital materials. Give a specific example of an error you caught and how you handled it. So for the last two summers, I worked at a market research firm, and my main part of the job was to ensure that over 500 documents got uploaded correctly to their website. Each document was a description of each product that they could provide, and I needed to make sure that all the formatting was correct. There were no grammar errors, that everything just looked consistent across the platform. So sometimes there would be incorrect fonts, or there would be something that was bolded that shouldn't be bolded as an example. And we kept like a very comprehensive spreadsheet, so I would just update the spreadsheet, and I would email the editing team and let them know this is what needs to be done. And I would continue to follow up until the errors were corrected. And then the last question asks when you find an issue, some you can fix yourself, and some need to go to developers as a bug ticket. How do you decide which is which and how do you write up an issue so that someone else can act on it without you asking questions? So I would obviously just take care of the ones that I knew I would be able to fix, perhaps ask a colleague, bounce an idea of them to confirm that what I was doing was correct. Otherwise if I needed to write up a ticket, I would make sure that everything was detail-oriented, listed out every single issue, and every single item that needed to be done, so that way they wouldn't have any questions. And that's pretty much it. Thanks so much for reviewing my video, and I look forward to hearing from you soon.
